official_source_notes.source: Java Language Specification, Java SE 21, chapter 15
official_source_notes.summary: Java integer operators use fixed primitive widths; integer overflow wraps silently except where a library method such as Math.*Exact specifies an exception.

official_source_notes.source: Java Language Specification, Java SE 21, sections 15.17.2 and 15.17.3
official_source_notes.summary: Integer division and remainder throw ArithmeticException on zero divisors; remainder has the dividend sign.

official_source_notes.source: Java Language Specification, Java SE 21, section 15.19
official_source_notes.summary: Java masks int shift counts to five low bits and long shift counts to six low bits.

official_source_notes.source: Java Language Specification, Java SE 21, section 5.1.3
official_source_notes.summary: Narrowing primitive conversions may lose high-order bits, precision or range.
Math.*Exact methods provide checked integer arithmetic and throw ArithmeticException on overflow.
Math.pow and Math.round expose floating-point math semantics that differ from pure fixed-width integer arithmetic.

generator.determinism: Native tool availability and compiler/runtime results are environment-dependent; --check validates shape and invariants rather than exact stdout.

official_source_notes.summary: Rust integer overflow checking depends on debug assertions and overflow-checks settings; overflow can panic or wrap depending on profile.

official_source_notes.source: Rust Reference: arithmetic and bitwise expressions
official_source_notes.summary: Division by zero, remainder by zero and invalid shifts are checked error cases; shift and arithmetic behavior is profile-sensitive for overflow.
Rust numeric casts use explicit `as` conversions with specified integer truncation or float conversion behavior.

official_source_notes.source: Rust standard library primitive integer methods
official_source_notes.summary: Primitive integer methods expose wrapping_*, checked_*, overflowing_* and saturating_* semantic families.

official_source_notes.source: Rust standard library primitive f64 methods
official_source_notes.summary: Floating methods such as powf, round and signum use f64 semantics before any explicit integer cast or profile-specific wrapper.
